Buyer's question

AI Implementation: Partner vs Internal Team Most teams should do both — sequentially.

Should you bring in an outside AI implementation partner or build the capability internally? At $5M-$50M B2B, the right answer is usually 'both, sequentially' — partner first to compress time-to-value, internal capability built during and after. Here's the decision framework.

Short answer

Partner first for the first 1-3 workflows; build internal capability during the engagement; transition to internal ownership at 90-180 days. Trying to start internally usually wastes 60-120 days on trial-and-error that a partner would compress. Trying to outsource everything indefinitely usually wastes money and creates dependency.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library

When to start with a partner

  • You haven't successfully shipped an AI workflow in production yet.
  • Your internal team doesn't have someone who has done this before.
  • You need rollout faster than internal trial-and-error allows (typical: 90 days with partner vs 6 months without).
  • You want to compress the political work — outside voice can move what internal politics blocks.
  • Budget allows \$5K-\$50K for fixed-fee implementation engagement.

When to start internally (no partner)

  • Someone on your team has shipped AI workflows at a previous company.
  • You have 4-6 months of patience for trial-and-error.
  • Budget is too tight for any outside help.
  • You're at <20 people and the founder can do this themselves.

The hybrid approach (most common right answer)

  1. Months 0-3: Partner-led implementation. Partner ships 1-3 workflows; trains the named internal owner alongside.
  2. Months 3-6: Joint operation. Partner advises; internal owner runs day-to-day. Partner present for major decisions.
  3. Months 6-12: Internal ownership. Partner on call for new initiatives. Quarterly check-ins.
  4. Months 12+: Fully internal unless major new initiative.

Cost comparison

The math usually favors the hybrid: partner compresses time-to-value, internal capability ensures sustainability.

Common mistakes

  • Hiring the partner without naming the internal owner. When the partner leaves, knowledge leaves with them.
  • Letting the partner do everything. Internal capability must be built during the engagement, not after.
  • Cutting partner short before transition is complete. Saves a month of fees; loses 6 months of compounding outcomes.
  • Extending the partner indefinitely. If you're still using the partner at 18 months, ownership has not transferred.

How to structure the partnership for handoff

  • Name the internal owner at engagement kickoff.
  • Internal owner attends every partner working session.
  • All Projects, prompts, and documentation lives in your systems (not the partner's).
  • Define handoff milestones in the engagement letter ("by week 8, internal owner runs workflow #1 solo").
  • Plan a clean exit date with quarterly check-in retainer after.

FAQ

How do I evaluate AI implementation partners?

Reference checks with similar clients; demand to see real deliverables from past engagements; ask about their handoff process.

Can we hire a dedicated internal AI engineer instead?

At <50 people, usually no — not enough work to justify. At 100+, sometimes.

Should we hire the partner's lead consultant as a full-time employee?

Sometimes works. Have the conversation openly; partners with high turnover view this as normal.

What's the right partner profile for our size?

B2B at \$5M-\$30M: small specialized firm (under 10 people). Larger orgs: mid-sized practice. Avoid Big-4 unless you specifically need the brand.

Can the partner help with hiring our internal AI lead?

Yes — good partners help spec and source the eventual full-time hire as part of the handoff plan.
